reviewNotes.summary: Thorough, well-structured contract with 27 criteria covering reply and forward commands, safety model parity with compose, threading, body/attachment handling, output formatting, error handling, help text, and regression. The safety model coverage is exhaustive -- 10 dedicated safety criteria (C-138 through C-144) mirror and extend Sprint 5's compose safety matrix. The safetyNotes section correctly delineates safe vs. unsafe testing domains per CLAUDE.md. Coverage against the sprint plan feature 'Reply & Forward' is complete.
strengths: Safety model criteria (C-138 to C-144) are comprehensive -- covering default-draft, send-with-flag, confirmation-prompt, --yes-without-send, and no-implicit-send. This mirrors Sprint 5's compose safety matrix with appropriate adaptations for two new commands.
C-154 requires parametrised safety tests covering at least 6 bypass scenarios per command, which is a strong architectural guarantee.
Reply-all recipient logic (C-133) is specific: tests user-address exclusion, sender-to-to promotion, and cc preservation. This is the trickiest piece of reply and it's well-specified.
C-135 tests original-content quoting for both reply and forward with attribution line verification -- this ensures the UX is correct, not just that the command runs.
Out-of-scope section is well-defined, correctly deferring HTML body, inline attachment forwarding, and signature management while noting which sprint owns each.
Regression sprints correctly lists [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] covering all prior work.
observations: No criterion explicitly tests that reply and forward auto-derive the subject from the original message (e.g. 'Re: Original Subject' for reply, 'Fwd: Original Subject' for forward). This is implied by the absence of --subject in test steps, and C-149 checks 'subject' appears in JSON output, but there is no explicit assertion on subject-prefix conventions. This is acceptable if Mail.app's native reply/forward constructs handle it, but could cause ambiguity if the implementation uses 'make new outgoing message' and must set the subject manually. The generator should handle this; no contract change needed.

C-133 tests user-address exclusion but doesn't specify how the user's address is determined (from the receiving account? from a config?). The test step uses 'user@example.com' as a placeholder. The generator will need to make an architectural choice here -- likely deriving it from the account that received the original message. This is an implementation detail, not a contract gap.

No criterion tests --from/--cc/--bcc as additional options on reply/forward (compose supports --from via C-112 and --cc/--bcc via C-108). This is acceptable for v1 -- reply/forward from the receiving account is the natural default, and extra recipients can be added in a future sprint if needed.

C-147 (threading) is necessarily vague because AppleScript threading can be achieved multiple ways (Mail.app 'reply' verb, In-Reply-To header, message reference). The threshold is pragmatic: 'includes a reference to the original message.' The evaluator should accept any approach that preserves threading semantics.

The regression registry (harness-state/regression/registry.json) currently only has sprints 1-4. Sprint 5 criteria will need to be added to the registry before this sprint's regression testing can cover Sprint 5. This is a process note, not a contract deficiency.
reviewNotes.safetyCompliance: All send-path criteria (C-140, C-141) use mock-only verification. Dry-run criteria (C-145, C-146) verify no AppleScript execution. No criterion requires or permits running --dangerously-send against real Mail.app. The safetyNotes section correctly references CLAUDE.md guardrails and defines safe read-only and write (draft-creation) testing domains. Fully compliant.
